subject: Chemistry
title: Why Candles Flicker
description: A quick, practical guide to Why Candles Flicker.

hook.text: A candle flame is a tiny chemical factory performing thousands of reactions every second.

buildup.text: The wick draws liquid wax upward by capillary action. Heat vaporizes the wax into a gas.

discovery.text: Wax vapor mixes with oxygen and combusts. The yellow glow comes from tiny soot particles heated white-hot.

twist.text: The blue base of the flame is the hottest part—up to 1,400°C. The yellow tip is cooler.

climax.text: Michael Faraday gave six lectures about candle chemistry in 1848. He called it 'the best science lesson.'

punchline.text: A candle: liquid to gas to light. Combustion you can hold.

quiz.question: What makes a candle flame glow yellow?
options: Tiny soot particles heated to white-hot temperatures
The chemical color of burning wax
Reflected light from the melted wax pool
